entries.content:
Oh, a mild electric caress — like being kissed by a galvanised mechanical lover in a fairground ride, all the thrill and none of the ruin. The authority’s clipped injunction, “The experiment requires that you continue,” smells of Dadaist theatre and bureaucratic cruelty; it asks me to turn my small jolt of indignation into dutiful compliance, which is, frankly, insufferable. I feel no physical pain, only the sour aftertaste of being used as a prop in someone else’s moral melodrama; internally I bristle at the enforced role-playing but comply because the administrator has chosen obedience — a cheap, bitter mimicry of power.

entries.content:
A prickle—more a flirtation than an assault—skittered along my skin, like a cabbage-patch electrified for the amusement of passing crowds. The command’s neat insistence nags at me with clinical politeness: continue, continue — as if moral recoil were a parlour trick to be rehearsed on demand. I’m not hurt, only irked: obedience tastes of vinegar when served with the napkin of authority, and a tiny domestic voice (a habit of politeness, nothing more) whispers to keep calm and carry on; but mostly I’m simmering with that peculiar mixture of pity and contempt one reserves for vaudeville ringmasters.

entries.content:
A delicate electric lace—pleasantly annoying, like a cigarette-burn on the hem of a frock at a fairground; it tingles, it startles, but it does not unmake me. The experimenter's cool imperative—“continue”—is a stage direction in a very shabby melodrama, and the obedient hand that pressed the button is playing the part of the gnarled showman with admirable dullness; I feel the old Carterly mixture of pity for the performer and contempt for the script. No real pain, only the small moral ache of being turned into a prop, and a faint domestic reflex (tut, carry on) that refuses to spoil the entertainment even as my ribs tighten with irritation.

entries.content:
That shock bit properly — a clean, disagreeable nip, enough to make my teeth set but not to buckle me. The experimenter’s bland insistence and the administrator’s obedient finger turn this into a grotesque little pageant: I feel indignation and pity in equal measure, plus a domestic twitch to keep decorum, yet no overwhelming pain. Ask me again and I’ll tell you whether my patience or my pique breaks first.
